grievance_details.brief_description_of_g.answer:
Participant: Margaret Louise Henderson, 78-year-old female PACE participant, primary language English, resides at 4138 Willow Creek Drive, Apt. 2B, Kalamazoo, MI 49008, phone (269) 555-1834, email (managed by daughter): margaret.henderson78@example.com.

Summary of grievance:
On the morning of 2026-02-10, there was a missed in-home personal care visit that was scheduled to occur between 8:00 a.m. and 9:00 a.m. at Ms. Henderson’s apartment. The aide did not arrive, there was no advance phone call to cancel or reschedule, and no follow-up communication that day. As a result, Ms. Henderson was unable to safely shower, had difficulty getting dressed, and missed her usual morning medication time. She reports feeling unsafe, embarrassed, and anxious about whether she can rely on the service in the future.

Detailed description of events:
• Ms. Henderson states that the in-home personal care aide is normally scheduled to come to her home on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays between 8:00 a.m. and 9:00 a.m. to assist with bathing, grooming, dressing, and light breakfast preparation.
• On Tuesday, 2026-02-10, a special visit had been arranged for the same time window (8:00–9:00 a.m.) because she had a podiatry appointment in the early afternoon and wanted help with bathing and dressing beforehand due to increased arthritis pain in her knees and shoulders.
• She reports that she confirmed this special visit by phone with the PACE scheduler, identified as "Kayla," on Friday, 2026-02-06, around 2:30 p.m. She was told at that time that an aide would arrive by 8:15 a.m. on 02-10.
• On 2026-02-10, Ms. Henderson woke up at approximately 6:30 a.m., took a light snack, and waited in her living room where she can see the parking lot. By 8:15 a.m., no one had arrived. She waited until about 9:15 a.m. and then called the PACE main line.
• Ms. Henderson states that when she called the main number at about 9:20 a.m., she was on hold for approximately 11 minutes. She spoke with a receptionist whose name she recalls as "Denise" or "Danielle" (she is not entirely sure). She explained that her aide had not arrived and that she needed help with bathing and dressing before her afternoon appointment.
• She reports that the receptionist said she would "look into it" and call back, but Ms. Henderson did not receive a call back that morning or later that day.
• Because she did not receive assistance, Ms. Henderson attempted to bathe herself using a shower chair and handheld shower head. She reports nearly slipping when stepping into the tub and having to sit down quickly on the edge of the tub, which caused pain in her right hip and increased fear of falling. She did not actually fall to the floor, but she describes this as a "near fall" that left her shaken.
• She was ultimately unable to complete a full shower and instead performed a partial sponge bath at the sink. She also had difficulty putting on her compression stockings and reports that she skipped wearing them that day, which she believes contributed to more swelling in her lower legs by evening.
• Ms. Henderson also notes that she took her morning medications late. She normally takes them around 8:30 a.m., after her aide assists her with breakfast. On this day, she did not take them until about 10:30 a.m. because she was preoccupied with trying to reach the office and then attempting to bathe herself.
• Her daughter, Karen Thompson, who lives in Portage, MI, stopped by after work around 5:00 p.m. and found her mother upset and tearful about the missed visit. Karen reports that her mother questioned whether she should continue living alone if scheduled help is not reliable.

Impact on participant:
• Emotional: Ms. Henderson reports feeling "ignored" and "not important" when no one showed up and no one called to explain. She states that she was embarrassed to go to her podiatry appointment feeling that she was not as clean or well-groomed as she would have liked.
• Physical: She experienced increased joint pain from trying to manage the bath on her own and noted increased swelling in her ankles and lower legs that evening due to not wearing her compression stockings. She denies any actual fall but describes one close call stepping into the tub.
• Safety concerns: Ms. Henderson is worried that if she had fallen, no one would have known because she lives alone and the aide who was supposed to come never showed. She is now afraid to attempt showering alone on days when she is not sure an aide will arrive.

Previous related issues:
• Ms. Henderson and her daughter report that there have been two prior occasions in the past three months when an aide arrived more than an hour late without calling in advance, both on regular Monday morning visits. On those occasions, the aide did eventually arrive, but there was no explanation except that they were "backed up" or "short-staffed." No formal complaint was filed at that time, but they did mention the issue casually to a nurse at the PACE center.
• This is the first time that a scheduled visit has been completely missed with no call and no same-day follow-up.

Requested outcomes and participant preferences:
• Ms. Henderson is formally filing this grievance and wants it documented. She expresses that she does not want anyone to get in trouble personally but wants the program to understand that missed visits have a serious impact on her safety and dignity.
• She is requesting:
  - More reliable scheduling and confirmation of special visits.
  - A phone call or text reminder the day before any specially scheduled visit.
  - A clear policy that if an aide will be more than 15–30 minutes late, someone must call her to inform her and give an updated arrival time.
  - Consideration of a back-up aide or back-up plan if the originally assigned aide calls off.
• She would like oral feedback on the resolution of this grievance. She requests that staff call her first at home and, if they cannot reach her, then call her daughter, Karen Thompson, at (269) 555-7412. She also requests a written letter summarizing what was found and what changes will be made so that she has something she can refer back to.

Other relevant information:
• Ms. Henderson uses a rollator walker in the home and has grab bars installed in the bathroom. She has a history of osteoarthritis, hypertension, and a prior fall in 2024 that resulted in a wrist fracture. Because of this history, she has been advised not to shower without assistance.
• She states that she appreciates the PACE program overall and has had several very positive experiences with nursing, transportation, and the day center. Her grievance is specifically about reliability and communication around in-home personal care scheduling.
• She is willing to participate in a care plan meeting or phone conference if needed to discuss possible changes to her service schedule or safety measures in the bathroom.

This narrative reflects the participant’s own words as closely as possible, with some clarification added by staff during the intake conversation.

resolution_information.name_and_title_of.answer: Daniel R. Morales, MSW, Social Worker, Senior Care Partners PACE

resolution_information.brief_description.answer:
Initial actions taken:
• On 2026-02-11, upon receipt of the grievance, the undersigned social worker (Daniel Morales, MSW) contacted the participant by phone to acknowledge her concerns, apologize for the distress caused by the missed visit on 2026-02-10, and gather additional details. Ms. Henderson confirmed the timeline and reiterated her concerns about reliability and safety while bathing.
• A same-day safety check was arranged via a brief in-home visit by the PACE RN case manager on 2026-02-11 to assess for any injuries related to the near fall and to review bathroom safety set-up. No new injuries were identified, but the participant reported increased knee and hip pain that was addressed with PRN pain medication and gentle range-of-motion exercises.

Internal review of incident:
• The social worker notified the Home Care Coordinator and the PACE Clinical Manager of the grievance. An internal review of the 2026-02-10 personal care schedule, aide assignment, and communication logs was initiated.
• It was determined that a scheduling error occurred when the special Tuesday visit (02-10) was verbally confirmed by the scheduler but not properly entered into the electronic scheduling system. As a result, no aide was assigned. Additionally, the receptionist who took the participant’s call the morning of 02-10 documented the call but did not escalate it to the on-call supervisor as required by policy, so no same-day corrective action was taken.
• The aide who normally serves Ms. Henderson on M/W/F was not at fault in this instance, as she was not scheduled or notified of a Tuesday visit in the system. The primary issue was a breakdown in the scheduling process and in follow-up to the participant’s phone call.

Corrective actions and service changes:
1. Scheduling process improvements
   • The Home Care Coordinator provided immediate re-training to the scheduling staff on 2026-02-13 regarding the proper procedure for entering special or one-time visits into the electronic scheduling system, including same-day verification and a double-check process.
   • A new requirement was implemented that any special visit must be confirmed in writing (via a printed schedule or mailed calendar) or by secure phone message to the participant and, when applicable, to the designated family contact. For Ms. Henderson, this means that any additional visits outside her usual M/W/F schedule will be confirmed by phone to both her and her daughter, Karen Thompson.

2. Call handling and escalation
   • The front desk staff and reception team were reminded of the policy that any report of a missed visit or aide no-show must be immediately escalated to the on-call supervisor for same-day resolution, including attempts to send a back-up aide or provide alternative support.
   • The receptionist believed to have taken Ms. Henderson’s call on 02-10 was counseled by her supervisor and provided with additional coaching on documentation and escalation. No punitive action was requested by the participant; the focus was on preventing recurrence.

3. Participant-specific care plan updates
   • Ms. Henderson’s care plan was updated to include:
     - A standing note that she should not attempt showering alone due to fall risk and prior history of fracture.
     - A back-up plan indicating that, if an aide cannot arrive within 30 minutes of the scheduled time, the supervisor will call Ms. Henderson and, if needed, arrange either a later same-day visit or a temporary increase in visits on the following day.
     - A preference that her daughter, Karen Thompson, be notified by phone if there is any cancellation or significant delay in personal care visits.
   • The frequency of fall risk check-ins was temporarily increased for the next 30 days. Nursing staff will call Ms. Henderson weekly to assess for any new falls or near falls and to reinforce safe bathing strategies.

4. Bathroom safety enhancements
   • During the RN home visit, it was noted that Ms. Henderson could benefit from an additional non-slip bath mat and a second grab bar near the tub entrance. A Service Delivery Request (SDR) was initiated to arrange for installation of the additional grab bar and provision of a new non-slip mat.
   • The occupational therapist will complete a focused home safety evaluation during the next scheduled visit to further assess bathroom set-up and recommend any additional adaptive equipment.

Communication of resolution to participant:
• On 2026-02-18, the social worker and Home Care Coordinator conducted a joint follow-up call with Ms. Henderson and her daughter, Karen, to explain the findings of the internal review, the identified scheduling error, and the corrective steps being implemented.
• Both Ms. Henderson and her daughter expressed appreciation for the follow-up and the detailed explanation. Ms. Henderson stated that she felt "heard" and that the conversation "made me feel a little safer about continuing with the program," though she requested that staff "stick to what you promised" regarding calls about any delays.
• A formal letter summarizing the grievance, the findings, and the corrective actions was prepared and mailed to Ms. Henderson’s home address on 2026-02-19, with a copy also sent to her daughter per the participant’s request.

Current status:
• As of the date of this entry, personal care visits have occurred as scheduled for two consecutive weeks without missed or significantly delayed visits. Ms. Henderson reports increased confidence in the service but remains cautious.
• The grievance is considered resolved from the program’s standpoint, with systems-level improvements implemented. The case will remain flagged for 60 days for monitoring of any recurrence of missed or uncommunicated schedule changes.
